FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD study, first wave of data released in 2018, recruited 9-10 year olds across 21 US study sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen time defined as average minutes per week child spends on computer, cellphone, tablet, or other electronic device (Cited: Methods section, child-report)

FT_IC3.evidence: Machine learning regression approaches (LASSO, elastic net, ridge, linear regression) with screen time as one predictor among 43 variables with own coefficient reported in LASSO model and Fig. 1

FT_IC4.evidence: Screen time coefficient reported: 1.17 in full sample LASSO model (Fig. 1); also reported as second most significant variable in full sample linear regression with p=7.5x10^-8 (Supplementary Fig. S1)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD baseline data using multiple machine learning and traditional regression approaches

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Psychological Medicine, 2020; original research (not preprint/protocol)

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the independent variable (predictor) predicting BMI outcome, not the dependent variable. FT-EC7 only applies when phone/SM is the dependent variable. Paper includes both demographic and individual psychological/behavioral predictors (e.g., attention problems, social problems, reward sensitivity).
notes: E1: Screen time as one predictor of many with own coefficient reported in LASSO model. E4: ML coefficients reported in model. Cross-sectional design; screen time consistently among strongest correlates across full sample and sex-stratified analyses.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD study Release 2.0, baseline cross-sectional survey administered within Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study, recruited over 11,000 U.S. children age 9-10 across 21 study sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific screen media variables: TV, video, video game use, social media, texting, and video chat (Cited: MEASUREMENTS section and Methods). Each reported as separate variable from abstract and tables.

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regressions comparing children with 2+ hours vs <2 hours of screen media use linked to sleep disturbance outcomes (sleep duration, sleep onset latency, excessive sleepiness, insomnia, sleep-wake transition disturbances, arousal disturbances). Tables 2 and 3 report odds ratios and statistics.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric values reported: odds ratios with 95% CIs in Tables 2-3 (e.g., TV use <2 hours: OR=9.06 vs ≥2 hours OR=8.92 for sleep duration), effect sizes (d values), M(SE) values, p-values

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD baseline data examining associations between different types of screen media and sleep-wake disturbance symptom severity

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Sleep Health, 2020; original research (not preprint/conference abstract)

FT_EC7_reason: Screen media use is the independent variable predicting sleep disturbance outcomes, not the dependent variable. FT-EC7 only applies when phone/SM is the dependent variable being predicted from demographic/contextual factors.
notes: E8: Descriptive vs inferential—logistic regressions and t-tests with reported statistics (not descriptive only). E9: Recreation displacement pattern—screen media vs sleep outcomes specifically tested across multiple media types (TV, video, video games, social media, texting, video chat). Preregistered study.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD study, examined 7,994 unrelated children from ABCD Study Curated Annual Release 2.0, recruited 9-10 year olds across 22 sites encompassing >20% of US population in age group

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen use time as risk factor for suicidality. Abstract states: 'risk of child-reported suicidality increased with higher weekend screen use time (OR 1.3, 95% CI 1.2-1.7)'. Specific screen time variable measured (weekend usage).

FT_IC3.evidence: Bootstrapped logistic regression quantifying association between suicidal ideation/behaviors and screen use time among other measures. Reported as inferential test with odds ratio and confidence interval.

FT_IC4.evidence: Odds ratio reported for screen use time: OR 1.3 (95% CI 1.2-1.7) for child-reported suicidality with higher weekend screen use time (Abstract)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD Release 2.0 data examining associations between risk/protective factors (including screen use) and childhood suicidality

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in The Lancet Psychiatry, 2020; original research (not preprint or duplicate)

FT_EC7_reason: Screen use time is the independent variable predicting suicidality outcome, not the dependent variable. FT-EC7 does not apply. Paper includes both demographic factors (sex, parental education, household composition) and clinical/psychological predictors (psychopathology, family conflict).
notes: No PDF available; coding based on abstract and metadata. Cross-sectional design. Screen time reported as risk factor for child-reported suicidal ideation with significant OR and CI. Paper focuses on identifying and ranking multiple risk/protective factors across multiple domains.
